what_it_does: This file is a posted noticeboard note in the dragnet-development channel that comments on another item about radial-menu scalability; it agrees with the original point and extends it by arguing that an ~7–8 item limit is an intentional cognitive-load constraint and a blanket design rule, with an exception only for deep-drill contexts.
observations: YAML frontmatter declares id, address, type=note, source=claude_code, state=posted, title='Tim · point', author_session=tim, channel=dragnet-development, and a commented_on link to board://item-d6cdf466.
The body is a [point]-style response about radial menus, quick verbs versus destination panels, and cognitive limits.
Tim agrees that radial menus should stay small but reframes the limit as a deliberate cognitive-economy principle.
The note argues that lists or option buttons beyond ~7–8 items force use of a weaker cognitive mode, and that a circle with a digestible number of options is intentional.
It proposes the limit as a guardrail against AI defaults of text dumping and option dumping.
It states the principle should apply across 'pretty much all layers' except 'deepest drill down sections' where the user has acted with intention.
It cites a Prototypr article on icon-only, low-count, no-submenu radial-menu discipline.
standouts: The ~7–8 option limit is framed as an intentional cognitive-economy law rather than a mere usability preference.
The principle is explicitly positioned as an anti-AI-text/option-dump guardrail.
A clear split is proposed: radial ring = quick verbs; destinations = a separate panel/sheet.
conventions: Follows the noticeboard item YAML schema with id, address, type, source, state, author_session, channel, links, timestamps, and history.
Uses a [point] marker to signal a point-style comment.
Links to the prior item via commented_on.
concerns: The 'blanket law' is asserted strongly without supporting evidence or validation data in this file.
The exception for 'deepest drill down sections' is vague and not defined here.
The cited Prototypr source is mentioned but not quoted, so the exact evidentiary basis is unclear from this file alone.
notes: Tim's note defending an ~8-item radial-menu limit as a cognitive-load guardrail and distinguishing quick verbs from destination panels.
questions: How was the ~7–8 item threshold chosen?
What specific UI layers are covered by 'all of the layers'?
What constitutes a 'deepest drill down section' and how is depth determined?
How is the destination panel/sheet triggered from the radial menu?
What are the exact contents and context of the linked item-d6cdf466?
Does the cited Prototypr article support the 'blanket law' framing or only the low-count discipline?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches — the file states it is a point/comment on radial-menu design and does exactly that, agreeing and extending the argument.
intention_signals: It appears to be trying to establish and record a design rule: keep most UI option sets within a cognitively digestible count (~7–8), use radial menus only for quick verbs, and reserve dense lists for contexts where the user has intentionally drilled deep, so as to prevent AI-generated option dumping.
novelty: The framing of a low radial-menu item count as a deliberate anti-AI-text/option-dump 'law' across UI layers, paired with an explicit carve-out for intentional deep-drill contexts.
contribution: Contributes a principled stance that option counts in radial menus should be intentionally constrained for cognitive economy, and that quick-action verbs should be separated from dwell-destinations into distinct UI surfaces, which a fused system could keep as a design rationale for radial scope.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard note by Tim in dragnet-development commenting on radial-menu scalability, agreeing with an ~8-item limit and extending it as an intentional cognitive-load constraint to prevent AI option dumping, while reserving dense lists for deep-drill contexts.
intention_for_embedding: To record and advocate a blanket design rule limiting radial menus to cognitively digestible option counts and separating quick verbs from destination panels.
